news_summary: Today's human rights news highlights significant progress in labor rights for gig workers, strengthened measures against child sexual abuse, increased understanding of human cooperation, and enhanced dialogue supporting pro-democracy and anti-war efforts.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité des droits de l'homme d'aujourd'hui met en avant des progrès significatifs en matière de droits du travail pour les travailleurs de la gig economy, le renforcement des mesures de lutte contre les abus sexuels sur mineurs, une meilleure compréhension de la coopération humaine, ainsi qu'un dialogue accru en faveur des efforts en faveur de la démocratie et contre la guerre.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy sobre derechos humanos destacan avances significativos en materia de derechos laborales para los trabajadores de la economía colaborativa, el refuerzo de las medidas contra el abuso sexual infantil, una mayor comprensión de la cooperación humana y un diálogo más intenso en apoyo de las iniciativas a favor de la democracia y contra la guerra.

articles.title: Historic ILO vote gives gig workers labour rights for the first time
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM For the first time, gig workers have binding international labour protections. The International Labour Organization voted June 12 to adopt a convention setting enforceable employment standards for platform workers in ride-hailing and food delivery. Four hundred and six member governments, employer groups, and workers’ representatives voted yes, including China, […]
The post Historic ILO vote gives gig workers labour rights for the first time first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

For the first time, gig workers have binding international labour protections.

The International Labour Organization voted June 12 to adopt a convention setting enforceable employment standards for platform workers in ride-hailing and food delivery. Four hundred and six member governments, employer groups, and workers’ representatives voted yes, including China, Japan, Germany, France, and South Africa among them. Eight voted no. The U.S. was one of them.

What does the convention cover?

The protections apply whether a worker is classified as an employee or an independent contractor. That classification has been the target for platform companies: call someone a contractor, and the minimum wage and benefits requirements disappear.

The convention sets minimum standards for occupational safety and health, minimum pay, and protection against unjustified termination or account deactivation. It also sets the first international rules on algorithmic management. Platforms must now disclose how and when automated systems control pay and access to work, something workers have been asking for since this industry came into existence.

Why the stakes are this high

The World Bank estimates between 154 million and 435 million people work through apps. A lot of them earn almost nothing once expenses come out. A 2025 Human Rights Watch report found U.S. platform workers surveyed earned a median of $5.12 per hour after expenses, about 30 percent below the federal minimum wage.

Amanda Brown, vice chair of the ILO’s Workers’ Group, put it directly: “For the first time in the history of international law, the women and men who move our cities, who clean and care in our homes… will be named, recognised and protected by a binding international standard.”

The real test: ratification and enforcement

The ILO can’t enforce anything on its own. Countries have to ratify the convention and write it into domestic law. This process is notoriously slow, political, and far from automatic. The U.S. voted against it. U.S. representative Lorenzo Riboni argued that rigid rules in a fast-moving sector “hinder innovation and harm workers that they intend to help.” Britain and India sat it out. European countries have a better track record on ratification, which is part of why the vote went the way it did.

What enforcement could look like

Where governments do ratify and implement the convention, individual workers may be able to take platform companies to court. The ILO can also receive complaints and pressure governments, but that’s roughly the extent of its reach. Lena Simet, senior advisor on economic justice at Human Rights Watch, called the result a breakthrough and “a floor, not a ceiling.” Governments still need to act: ratify, enforce correct classification, and close gaps for workers misclassified as self-employed. The standard is set. Everything after this is implementation.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a historic and binding international labour rights convention for gig workers, setting enforceable standards that protect millions globally. This represents a significant positive impact on workers' rights and dignity, with broad international support and detailed protections against exploitation, including algorithmic management transparency. The story is focused, substantial, and meaningful for a large segment of the global workforce.
articles.personality_title: International Labour Organization sets first global labour rights for gig workers
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Millions of people around the world work as gig workers, such as drivers for ride apps or food delivery couriers. Until now, they often lacked clear legal protections because companies called them independent contractors, not employees.

**What happened** – On June 12, the International Labour Organization (ILO) voted to create the first international rules that protect gig workers. These rules apply to workers in ride-hailing and food delivery platforms. The vote included 406 members from governments, employers, and workers, with countries like China, Japan, Germany, France, and South Africa in favor. The United States voted against the new rules.

**Impact** – This new convention sets minimum standards for pay, safety, and protection from unfair firing. It also requires companies to explain how they use automated systems to control work and pay. This is important because many gig workers earn very little after expenses, sometimes less than minimum wage. The rules give workers legal rights no matter how companies classify them.

**What’s next step** – The ILO itself cannot enforce these rules. Each country must agree to follow them by adding the rules into their own laws. This process can be slow and political. If countries do ratify the convention, workers could take companies to court if rights are broken. The new rules are a first step, but governments still need to act to protect gig workers fully.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, gig workers worldwide have a clear set of international labour rights that protect their pay, safety, and job security, marking a historic change in work protections.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’Organisation internationale du travail établit les premiers droits du travail mondiaux pour les travailleurs indépendants
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des millions de personnes dans le monde travaillent comme travailleurs indépendants, par exemple conducteurs d’applications de transport ou livreurs de repas. Jusqu’à présent, ils manquaient souvent de protections légales claires car les entreprises les considéraient comme des contractants indépendants, pas des employés.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 12 juin, l’Organisation internationale du travail (OIT) a voté pour créer les premières règles internationales protégeant les travailleurs indépendants. Ces règles concernent les travailleurs des plateformes de transport et de livraison de nourriture. Le vote a réuni 406 membres issus de gouvernements, employeurs et représentants des travailleurs, dont la Chine, le Japon, l’Allemagne, la France et l’Afrique du Sud. Les États-Unis ont voté contre.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle convention fixe des normes minimales de salaire, de sécurité et de protection contre un licenciement injustifié. Elle oblige aussi les entreprises à expliquer comment elles utilisent des systèmes automatisés pour gérer le travail et le paiement. C’est important car beaucoup de travailleurs gagnent très peu après leurs dépenses, parfois moins que le salaire minimum. Ces règles donnent des droits juridiques aux travailleurs, quelle que soit leur classification.

**Prochaine étape** – L’OIT ne peut pas appliquer ces règles elle-même. Chaque pays doit accepter de les suivre en les intégrant dans ses propres lois. Ce processus peut être long et politique. Si les pays ratifient la convention, les travailleurs pourront poursuivre les entreprises en justice si leurs droits sont violés. Ces règles sont une première étape, mais les gouvernements doivent encore agir pour protéger pleinement les travailleurs indépendants.

**En une phrase** – Pour la première fois, les travailleurs indépendants dans le monde disposent d’un ensemble clair de droits du travail internationaux protégeant leur salaire, leur sécurité et leur emploi, marquant un changement historique.
articles.personality_title_es: La Organización Internacional del Trabajo establece los primeros derechos laborales globales para trabajadores de plataformas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Millones de personas en todo el mundo trabajan como trabajadores de plataformas, como conductores de aplicaciones de transporte o repartidores de comida. Hasta ahora, a menudo carecían de protecciones legales claras porque las empresas los clasificaban como contratistas independientes, no como empleados.

**Qué pasó** – El 12 de junio, la Organización Internacional del Trabajo (OIT) votó para crear las primeras reglas internacionales que protegen a los trabajadores de plataformas. Estas reglas aplican a trabajadores de transporte y entrega de alimentos. En la votación participaron 406 miembros entre gobiernos, empleadores y representantes de trabajadores, incluyendo países como China, Japón, Alemania, Francia y Sudáfrica. Estados Unidos votó en contra.

**Impacto** – Esta nueva convención establece estándares mínimos para el pago, la seguridad y la protección contra despidos injustificados. También exige que las empresas expliquen cómo usan sistemas automáticos para controlar el trabajo y el pago. Esto es importante porque muchos trabajadores ganan muy poco después de gastos, a veces menos que el salario mínimo. Las reglas dan derechos legales a los trabajadores sin importar cómo los clasifiquen.

**Próximo paso** – La OIT no puede hacer cumplir estas reglas por sí sola. Cada país debe aceptar seguirlas incorporándolas en sus propias leyes. Este proceso puede ser lento y político. Si los países ratifican la convención, los trabajadores podrían demandar a las empresas si se violan sus derechos. Estas reglas son un primer paso, pero los gobiernos aún deben actuar para proteger completamente a los trabajadores de plataformas.

**Una frase clave** – Por primera vez, los trabajadores de plataformas en todo el mundo tienen un conjunto claro de derechos laborales internacionales que protegen su salario, seguridad y estabilidad laboral, marcando un cambio histórico.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a diverse group of glowing, interconnected gears shaped like delivery bags, ride-hailing cars, and mobile phones, symbolizing gig workers united and protected by a sturdy, transparent shield made of woven threads representing binding international labor rights and fairness, set against a soft, natural background of gentle earth tones.

articles.title: Press release - Combating child sexual abuse: agreement on updated rules
articles.summary: To combat child sexual abuse crimes more effectively, an updated EU directive criminalises new technological offences and extends the limitation periods for prosecuting crimes.Committee on Civil Liberties, Justice and Home Affairs Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
On Monday, Parliament and Council negotiators agreed on modernising the EU legal framework for combating child sexual abuse (CSA) crimes, including updated definitions, higher maximum punishments, longer limitation periods for prosecuting crimes, and better victim support.



Criminalising new offences



Taking into account technological developments, the updated law criminalises new offences, like producing or disseminating instruction manuals for CSA crimes including exploitation and solicitation (to be punishable by at least two years’ imprisonment).

MEPs pushed for the criminalisation of artificial intelligence systems designed or adapted specifically to produce child sexual abuse material. Going forward, acquiring, possessing or disseminating such systems will be punishable by up to two years’ imprisonment. MEPs also ensured that organising another person’s travel to another country for the purpose of abuse will be criminalised (by up to one year).

Unlike the existing directive, the new law also addresses grooming, i.e. the solicitation of children for sexual purposes. Proposing to meet a child under the age of consent will be punishable when it leads to offences (up to one year, or two years when coercion, force or threat is involved). Attempted solicitation, including online, for a child to provide child sexual abuse material (CSAM) will also become punishable (by up to six months, or one year with coercion, force or threat). Other newly criminalised offences include livestreaming CSA and sextortion (threatening children with the release of CSAM).



Penalties and the notion of consent



The new law will increase the maximum penalties for a number of CSA crimes, for example knowingly attending a CSA performance involving a child, and sexual activities with children who are exploited in prostitution. In future, several offences involving CSAM will also carry stricter punishments, for instance acquiring, possessing or knowingly obtaining access to CSAM (at least two years’ imprisonment) and distributing, disseminating, supplying and making available CSAM (at least three years).

To address the spread of CSAM online, EU countries need to ensure that such material hosted on their territory can be promptly removed, or that internet users in their territory are blocked from accessing it.

MEPs also pushed for strengthening the notion of consent in CSA legislation. In future, penetrative sexual activities with children above the age of consent who do not consent will be punishable by up to 10 years’ imprisonment. For children below the age of consent, this rises to 12 years.



Longer periods to report abuse and start investigations



Victims of CSA crimes typically need years or decades to come forward and report the crimes. Therefore, MEPs pushed to extend the statute of limitations on CSA crimes.

For the most serious offences (for example, penetrative sexual activities and coercing children under the age of consent into prostitution), punishable by at least 10 years’ imprisonment, limitation periods will be set at 32 years from the victim reaching the age of majority. For the majority of offences covered by the legislation and punishable by 5 to 10 years (for example), the limitation period will be 20 years. For a third group of offences, including the production of child sexual abuse material, it will be 15 years.



Prevention and victims’ rights



The law ensures that member states put in place specialised and appropriate support for victims (recognised as “survivors” of abuse). EU countries will strive to ensure that child victims have access to assessment, support and protection in a child-friendly setting, and where possible, on the same premises. Victims will have also have the right to claim compensation from offenders, and helplines must be available to inform people of the support available.

Information campaigns will highlight the fact that non-consensual sex is a criminal offence, and that consent must be given freely. Also, effective intervention programmes for offenders during or after criminal proceedings must be put in place to prevent repeated offences.

The law also improves reporting for CSA crimes, ensuring that professional secrecy rules do not prevent reporting when there are reasonable grounds to suspect abuse. Professionals working in close contact with children will have an obligation to report cases where they have “substantial grounds” to believe that the wellbeing of the child is in “serious and imminent danger” from a CSA crime.

The law also strengthens organisations working in child protection. Member states must ensure there is a legal basis for them to manage hotlines and helplines. They are also encouraged to allow organisations to search for publicly accessible CSAM on hosting services.



Background checks



When recruiting for positions involving regular contact with children, employers need to conduct background checks to ensure that the applicant has not been convicted of CSA crimes.

Following a push from MEPs, this provision was extended to include organised voluntary activities involving children (for example, after-school activities).



Quote



Rapporteur Jeroen Lenaers (EPP, the Netherlands) said: «We listened to victims, and it became clear that we needed to update our laws to reflect their experiences and the challenges of today. With this agreement, we managed to significantly extend the limitation periods for serious crimes. The law also addresses grooming and the spread of abuse material online, and ensures that law enforcement can combat new forms of abuse – including AI systems and instruction manuals. With stricter punishments, better victim support and a stronger role for child protection NGOs, EU countries will have the right tools in place to make sure that justice is done.”



Next steps



Technical teams will now finalise the legal text.

Then, both Parliament and Council will need to formally adopt the law before it can be published in the EU Official Journal. EU countries will then have three years to transpose the provisions of the directive into national law.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant update to the EU legal framework to combat child sexual abuse, including criminalizing new technological offences, extending limitation periods for prosecution, increasing penalties, and improving victim support. These changes have broad societal impact, addressing a critical human rights issue with detailed measures and clear benefits for victims and society.
articles.personality_title: EU updates laws to better fight child sexual abuse and support victims
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Child sexual abuse is a serious crime that can take many forms, including new ones linked to technology. The European Union (EU) wanted to improve its laws to better protect children and help victims.

**What happened** – On Monday, the EU Parliament and Council agreed on new rules to fight child sexual abuse. These rules add punishments for new crimes like using artificial intelligence (AI) to create abusive material, grooming children online, and organizing travel for abuse. The law also increases penalties for many offenses and extends the time victims have to report crimes. It requires better support for victims and stronger checks on people working with children.

**Impact** – These changes are important because they respond to new challenges such as AI misuse and online abuse that were not covered before. Extending the time to report crimes acknowledges that victims often need many years to speak up. The law also makes sure victims get help in a child-friendly way and that organizations protecting children get more support. This means better chances for justice and safety.

**What's next step** – Experts will finish the final legal text. Then, the EU Parliament and Council will officially approve the law. After that, EU countries have three years to include these rules in their own laws.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The EU has strengthened its laws to fight child sexual abuse by criminalizing new offenses, extending reporting times, increasing penalties, and improving victim support across member countries.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'UE renforce ses lois pour mieux lutter contre les abus sexuels sur enfants et soutenir les victimes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les abus sexuels sur enfants sont des crimes graves, qui prennent aussi de nouvelles formes liées à la technologie. L'Union européenne (UE) a voulu améliorer ses lois pour mieux protéger les enfants et aider les victimes.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Lundi, le Parlement et le Conseil de l'UE ont convenu de nouvelles règles pour lutter contre les abus sexuels sur enfants. Ces règles punissent de nouveaux crimes comme l’utilisation de l’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour créer du contenu abusif, le démarchage en ligne des enfants (grooming) et l’organisation de voyages pour des abus. La loi augmente aussi les peines pour plusieurs infractions et prolonge le délai pour signaler les crimes. Elle exige un meilleur soutien aux victimes et des contrôles renforcés pour les personnes en contact avec des enfants.

**Impact** – Ces changements sont importants car ils répondent à de nouveaux défis, comme l’usage abusif de l’IA et les abus en ligne, qui n’étaient pas couverts avant. Prolonger le délai de signalement reconnaît que les victimes ont souvent besoin de beaucoup de temps pour parler. La loi garantit aussi un soutien adapté aux enfants et renforce les organisations de protection. Cela offre de meilleures chances de justice et de sécurité.

**Prochaine étape** – Des experts finaliseront le texte juridique. Ensuite, le Parlement et le Conseil de l’UE adopteront officiellement la loi. Puis, les pays membres auront trois ans pour intégrer ces règles dans leurs lois nationales.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’UE a renforcé ses lois pour lutter contre les abus sexuels sur enfants en criminalisant de nouveaux délits, en allongeant les délais de signalement, en augmentant les peines et en améliorant le soutien aux victimes dans tous les pays membres.
articles.personality_title_es: La UE actualiza leyes para combatir mejor el abuso sexual infantil y apoyar a las víctimas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El abuso sexual infantil es un delito grave que puede presentarse de muchas formas, incluidas nuevas relacionadas con la tecnología. La Unión Europea (UE) quiso mejorar sus leyes para proteger mejor a los niños y ayudar a las víctimas.

**Qué pasó** – El lunes, el Parlamento y el Consejo de la UE acordaron nuevas normas para combatir el abuso sexual infantil. Estas normas castigan nuevos delitos como el uso de inteligencia artificial (IA) para crear material abusivo, el acoso a niños en línea (grooming) y la organización de viajes para abusos. La ley también aumenta las penas para varios delitos y extiende el tiempo que tienen las víctimas para denunciar. Exige mejor apoyo a las víctimas y controles más estrictos a quienes trabajan con niños.

**Impacto** – Estos cambios son importantes porque responden a nuevos desafíos como el mal uso de la IA y los abusos en línea que antes no se contemplaban. Extender el plazo para denunciar reconoce que las víctimas suelen necesitar muchos años para hablar. La ley también asegura apoyo adaptado para los niños y fortalece a las organizaciones de protección. Esto significa mejores oportunidades para la justicia y la seguridad.

**Próximo paso** – Expertos finalizarán el texto legal. Luego, el Parlamento y el Consejo de la UE aprobarán oficialmente la ley. Después, los países de la UE tendrán tres años para incluir estas normas en sus propias leyes.

**Resumen en una frase** – La UE ha fortalecido sus leyes para combatir el abuso sexual infantil criminalizando nuevos delitos, extendiendo los tiempos para denunciar, aumentando las penas y mejorando el apoyo a las víctimas en todos los países miembros.
articles.image_prompt: A protective shield made of interlocking puzzle pieces surrounds a glowing, stylized child figure made of light, while shadowy, abstract shapes representing threats—such as a broken mask and a digital spider web—are held back outside the shield; the scene is rendered as a warm, detailed painting with soft earth tones and gentle blues, symbolizing strengthened laws, victim support, and safeguarding children from abuse.

articles.title: A study of 100,000 people found we cooperate more than we think
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM In a standardized behavioral experiment run with more than 100,000 people across 125 countries, 69 percent of participants chose to cooperate with an anonymous stranger on behalf of a shared goal, even when doing so meant taking a personal financial loss. Those same participants, when asked how many of […]
The post A study of 100,000 people found we cooperate more than we think first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

In a standardized behavioral experiment run with more than 100,000 people across 125 countries, 69 percent of participants chose to cooperate with an anonymous stranger on behalf of a shared goal, even when doing so meant taking a personal financial loss. Those same participants, when asked how many of their fellow citizens would make the same choice, guessed 47 percent.

The research, led by a German team and published in Science, is the first globally representative study of human cooperation. Participants were drawn from 125 nationally representative country samples.

What the experiment measured

Each participant was paired with an anonymous person from their own country and given a choice. The non-cooperative option guaranteed $100. The cooperative option paid only $70, but if both parties independently chose it, $400 would be donated toward climate action. Choosing cooperation meant accepting a $30 personal loss in exchange for a $400 contribution to a common cause, contingent on a stranger making the same call without knowing the other had.

On average, 69 percent of participants cooperated. That figure held consistently across the full range of countries in the sample.

The gap between what people do and what they expect

The same participants were asked to estimate what share of their fellow citizens would cooperate. The average guess was 47 percent. That 22-point gap between actual behavior and expected behavior appeared in 124 of 125 countries surveyed, making it nearly universal.

Lead author Armin Falk at the University of Bonn put the implication directly: “If we were less pessimistic and therefore more realistic, we could live in a better world.” The researchers describe the gap as a form of cognitive self-deception, a tendency to assess others too negatively while behaving more generously than expected. “And in doing so, we weaken ourselves.”

Why this matters beyond the laboratory

Collective action on problems that require coordination, from climate change to public health, depends in part on what people believe others will do. If most people assume most others will not participate, the incentive to participate weakens. The pessimism becomes self-fulfilling.

The researchers frame cooperation as a basic prerequisite for social well-being. Many challenges can only be overcome if people contribute to the common good beyond their immediate interests. The data suggests most people already do. They just do not know that most other people are doing the same.

Source study: Science— Homo cooperans: Understanding the nature of human cooperation

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a large-scale, globally representative study demonstrating that a majority of people cooperate for common good even at personal cost, revealing a positive and hopeful insight into human behavior that can enhance collective action on major societal challenges like climate change. The study’s scope, significance, and detailed findings provide meaningful context and substance.
articles.personality_title: Global study shows most people cooperate more than they expect
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists wanted to learn how often people work together for a shared goal, even if it costs them something. They studied 100,000 people from 125 countries to get a clear picture of human cooperation around the world.

**What happened** – In the experiment, each person had to choose between keeping $100 or accepting $70 while trying to help a stranger. If both people chose the smaller amount, $400 would be donated to fight climate change. About 69% of participants decided to cooperate, even though it meant they earned less money. However, when asked how many others would cooperate, participants guessed only 47%.

**Impact** – This study is the first to show, on a global scale, that most people are willing to work together and make personal sacrifices for the greater good. It also revealed that people underestimate how many others are willing to do the same. This gap between what people do and what they expect can stop cooperation from growing, because people don’t realize how common generosity really is.

**What's next step** – Researchers suggest that if people understood how much others cooperate, they might feel more encouraged to join in themselves. This could help solve big problems like climate change and public health by increasing teamwork and trust.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Most people worldwide choose to cooperate for common goals, but they don’t realize how many others do the same, which limits even greater cooperation.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une étude mondiale révèle que la plupart des gens coopèrent plus qu’ils ne le pensent
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des scientifiques ont voulu savoir à quelle fréquence les gens travaillent ensemble pour un objectif commun, même si cela leur coûte quelque chose. Ils ont étudié 100 000 personnes dans 125 pays pour mieux comprendre la coopération humaine dans le monde.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Dans l’expérience, chaque personne devait choisir entre garder 100 $ ou accepter 70 $ en essayant d’aider un inconnu. Si les deux choisissaient la somme plus faible, 400 $ étaient donnés pour lutter contre le changement climatique. Environ 69 % des participants ont choisi de coopérer, même si cela leur rapportait moins d’argent. Mais quand on leur a demandé combien d’autres coopéreraient, ils ont estimé seulement 47 %.

**Impact** – Cette étude est la première à montrer à l’échelle mondiale que la majorité des gens sont prêts à travailler ensemble et à faire des sacrifices personnels pour le bien commun. Elle a aussi révélé que les gens sous-estiment combien d’autres sont prêts à faire de même. Cette différence entre ce que les gens font et ce qu’ils pensent peut freiner la coopération, car ils ne réalisent pas à quel point la générosité est répandue.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs suggèrent que si les gens comprenaient mieux combien d’autres coopèrent, ils seraient plus encouragés à participer eux-mêmes. Cela pourrait aider à résoudre de grands problèmes comme le changement climatique et la santé publique en renforçant la confiance et le travail d’équipe.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La plupart des gens dans le monde choisissent de coopérer pour des objectifs communs, mais ils ne réalisent pas combien d’autres font pareil, ce qui limite une coopération encore plus grande.
articles.personality_title_es: Un estudio mundial muestra que la mayoría coopera más de lo que cree
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos querían saber con qué frecuencia las personas trabajan juntas por un objetivo común, incluso si eso les cuesta algo. Estudiaron a 100,000 personas de 125 países para entender mejor la cooperación humana en todo el mundo.

**Qué pasó** – En el experimento, cada persona debía elegir entre quedarse con 100 dólares o aceptar 70 dólares tratando de ayudar a un desconocido. Si ambos elegían la cantidad menor, se donaban 400 dólares para combatir el cambio climático. Alrededor del 69 % de los participantes eligió cooperar, aunque ganaran menos dinero. Pero cuando les preguntaron cuántos otros cooperarían, estimaron solo el 47 %.

**Impacto** – Este estudio es el primero que muestra a nivel mundial que la mayoría de las personas están dispuestas a trabajar juntas y hacer sacrificios personales por el bien común. También reveló que la gente subestima cuántos otros están dispuestos a hacer lo mismo. Esta diferencia entre lo que la gente hace y lo que espera puede frenar la cooperación, porque no saben lo común que es la generosidad.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores sugieren que si las personas entendieran cuánto cooperan otros, se sentirían más animadas a participar. Esto podría ayudar a resolver grandes problemas como el cambio climático y la salud pública aumentando la confianza y el trabajo en equipo.

**Resumen en una frase** – La mayoría de las personas en el mundo eligen cooperar por objetivos comunes, pero no saben cuántos otros hacen lo mismo, lo que limita una cooperación aún mayor.
articles.image_prompt: Two pairs of hands, each pair gently passing a glowing seedling between them across a small gap, symbolizing anonymous cooperation; behind the hands, a softly lit globe made of interlocking puzzle pieces representing 125 countries, with warm, natural earth tones and a subtle golden light connecting the elements, conveying trust, shared sacrifice, and collective action for a better world.

articles.title: Press release - Brussels dialogue with Russian pro-democracy and anti-war actors
articles.summary: On Tuesday, the European Parliament will host a conference where participants will explore the prospects and challenges for pro-democracy players in Russia.Committee on Foreign Affairs Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
WHEN: Tuesday 23 June (opening session 9.00-10.15)

WHERE: European Parliament, Brussels







Members of Russian democratic forces, anti-war activists, civil society representatives and academics will get together in Brussels with representatives of EU institutions, EU members states and international partners and experts on Tuesday. The high-level conference, hosted by the European Parliament, with the support of the European Commission and the European External Action Service (EEAS), is the second edition of the "Brussels Dialogue" with Russian democratic actors. The first took place in June 2023.

The event aims to provide a platform for dialogue and cooperation, encouraging exchanges among different elements of Russian civil society, academia and democratic activism while fostering a deeper mutual understanding.

At the opening, the President of the European Parliament, Roberta Metsola, will address the participants via video message. European Parliament Vice-President Pina Picierno, Commissioners for Defence and Enlargement Andrius Kubilus and Marta Kos, and UN Special Rapporteur on the situation of human rights in the Russian Federation Mariana Katzarova, will also speak. Svetlana Gannushkina, human rights activist and founder and chair of the Civic Assistance Committee, and Dimitry Muratov, Nobel Peace Prize laureate and former editor-in-chief of Novaya Gazeta, will address the participants during the opening session.

Across several panels, participants will go on to discuss the challenges that Russian society is facing in the field of human rights, the long-term implications of Russia’s war of aggression against Ukraine, and disinformation. The panellists will also explore how to work towards a just and sustainable peace for Ukraine.







Important information

Only the opening session, 9.00-10.15 CET, can be followed here. The rest of the conference will be held in camera.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a high-level conference hosted by the European Parliament that brings together Russian pro-democracy and anti-war actors, EU representatives, and human rights experts to discuss challenges in Russian society, human rights issues, and prospects for peace in Ukraine. This event promotes dialogue, cooperation, and mutual understanding with potential broad societal and geopolitical impact, especially regarding human rights and peace efforts.
articles.personality_title: European Parliament hosts dialogue with Russian pro-democracy and anti-war groups
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The European Parliament is holding a conference in Brussels to bring together Russian pro-democracy activists, anti-war groups, and experts. This is the second time such a meeting, called the "Brussels Dialogue," is taking place, following the first event in 2023. 

**What happened** – On Tuesday, June 23, members of Russian civil society, academics, EU representatives, and international experts gathered to talk about the challenges facing Russian society, human rights, and the impact of Russia’s war against Ukraine. The event included speeches from important figures like the European Parliament President Roberta Metsola, human rights activist Svetlana Gannushkina, and Nobel Peace Prize winner Dmitry Muratov. Several panels discussed how to promote peace and fight disinformation.

**Impact** – This conference is important because it creates a space for open dialogue between Russian activists and European leaders. It helps build understanding and cooperation on difficult issues like human rights and the war in Ukraine. Bringing together different voices can support efforts for peace and democracy in a time of conflict.

**What's next step** – The discussions from this event could influence future policies and support programs for Russian civil society and peace efforts. The conference will continue privately after the opening session, allowing deeper conversations among participants to explore solutions and cooperation.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Brussels Dialogue brings Russian pro-democracy and anti-war voices together with European leaders to discuss challenges and seek paths toward peace and human rights improvements.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen accueille un dialogue avec des groupes russes pro-démocratie et anti-guerre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Parlement européen organise une conférence à Bruxelles réunissant des militants russes pro-démocratie, des groupes anti-guerre et des experts. C’est la deuxième édition du "Dialogue de Bruxelles", après la première en 2023.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le mardi 23 juin, des membres de la société civile russe, des universitaires, des représentants de l’UE et des experts internationaux se sont réunis pour discuter des défis de la société russe, des droits humains et des conséquences de la guerre de la Russie contre l’Ukraine. Des personnalités importantes comme la présidente du Parlement européen Roberta Metsola, l’activiste des droits humains Svetlana Gannushkina et le lauréat du prix Nobel de la paix Dmitry Muratov ont pris la parole. Plusieurs panels ont abordé la promotion de la paix et la lutte contre la désinformation.

**Impact** – Cette conférence est importante car elle crée un espace de dialogue ouvert entre activistes russes et dirigeants européens. Elle favorise la compréhension et la coopération sur des sujets difficiles comme les droits humains et la guerre en Ukraine. Rassembler différentes voix peut soutenir les efforts pour la paix et la démocratie en période de conflit.

**Prochaine étape** – Les discussions pourront influencer les futures politiques et les programmes d’aide à la société civile russe et aux efforts de paix. La conférence se poursuivra à huis clos après la séance d’ouverture, permettant des échanges plus approfondis.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Dialogue de Bruxelles réunit les voix pro-démocratie et anti-guerre russes avec les dirigeants européens pour discuter des défis et chercher des voies vers la paix et l’amélioration des droits humains.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo acoge un diálogo con grupos rusos pro-democracia y anti-guerra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Parlamento Europeo organiza una conferencia en Bruselas que reúne a activistas rusos pro-democracia, grupos anti-guerra y expertos. Esta es la segunda edición del "Diálogo de Bruselas", tras la primera en 2023.

**Qué pasó** – El martes 23 de junio, miembros de la sociedad civil rusa, académicos, representantes de la UE y expertos internacionales se reunieron para hablar sobre los desafíos en la sociedad rusa, los derechos humanos y las consecuencias de la guerra de Rusia contra Ucrania. Figuras importantes como la presidenta del Parlamento Europeo, Roberta Metsola, la activista de derechos humanos Svetlana Gannushkina y el premio Nobel de la Paz Dmitry Muratov dieron discursos. Varias mesas redondas trataron sobre cómo promover la paz y combatir la desinformación.

**Impacto** – Esta conferencia es importante porque crea un espacio de diálogo abierto entre activistas rusos y líderes europeos. Ayuda a construir entendimiento y cooperación en temas difíciles como los derechos humanos y la guerra en Ucrania. Reunir diferentes voces puede apoyar los esfuerzos por la paz y la democracia en tiempos de conflicto.

**Próximo paso** – Las discusiones podrían influir en futuras políticas y programas de apoyo a la sociedad civil rusa y los esfuerzos de paz. La conferencia continuará a puerta cerrada después de la sesión de apertura para permitir conversaciones más profundas.

**Resumen en una frase** – El Diálogo de Bruselas reúne voces pro-democracia y anti-guerra rusas con líderes europeos para discutir desafíos y buscar caminos hacia la paz y la mejora de los derechos humanos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a round wooden table set in a softly lit European Parliament chamber, with symbolic elements including intertwined olive branches representing peace, open books and quills symbolizing dialogue and academia, and two doves gently facing each other above the table, all rendered in natural, muted colors to evoke hope and cooperation among diverse voices.
